The generative approach to [[second-language acquisition|second language (L2) acquisition]] (SLA) is a cognitive based theory of SLA that applies theoretical insights developed from within [[generative linguistics]] to investigate how second languages and dialects are acquired and lost by individuals learning naturalistically or with formal instruction in foreign, second language and lingua franca settings.<ref name="ellis_r">Ellis, R. (1994): [https://books.google.com/books?id=3KglibyrZ5sC The Study of Second Language Acquisition]. Oxford: Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>Gass, S. M. and Selinker, L. (2001): Second Language Acquisition: An Introductory Course. Second edition. Mahwah, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.</ref><ref>Gregg, K. R. (1994): Second language acquisition: history and theory. Encyclopedia of Language and Linguistics.Second edition. Oxford: Pergamon, 3720–6.</ref><ref>Jordens, P. and Lalleman, J. (eds) (1988):Language Development. Dordrecht:Foris.</ref><ref>Ritchie, W. R. and Bhatia, T. J. (eds) (1996): Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. San Diego: Academic Press.</ref><ref>Towell, R. and Hawkins, R. (1994):Approaches to Second Language Acquisition. Clevedon: Multilingual Matters.</ref><ref name=":0">Doughty, C.J., Long, M.H. (2003). [https://books.google.com/books?id=xmLoVScagwYC&q=generative The Handbook of Second Language Acquisition]. Oxford: Blackwell Publishing.</ref> Central to generative linguistics is the concept of [[Universal Grammar]] (UG), a part of an innate, biologically endowed language faculty which refers to knowledge alleged to be common to all human languages.<ref name=":3">Chomsky, N. (1965). Aspects of the Theory of Syntax. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.</ref><ref name=":4">Chomsky, N. (1981). Lectures on Government and Binding. Dordrecht:Foris.</ref><ref name=":5">Pinker, S. (1994). The Language Instinct.New York: William Morrow.</ref><ref name=":2">White, L. (2003). On the Nature of Interlanguage Representation: Universal Grammar in the Second Language. In C.J. Doughty and M.C. Long (eds) The Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. Oxford: Blackwell Publishing 19-42</ref><ref name=":1" /> UG includes both invariant principles as well as parameters that allow for variation which place limitations on the form and operations of grammar.<ref name=":2" /> Subsequently, research within the Generative Second-Language Acquisition (GenSLA) tradition describes and explains SLA by probing the interplay between Universal Grammar, knowledge of one's [[first language|native language]] and input from the [[language education|target language]].<ref name=":1" /> Research is conducted in [[syntax]], [[phonology]], [[Morphology (linguistics)|morphology]], [[phonetics]], [[semantics]], and has some relevant applications to [[pragmatics]].

Some of the main questions in GenSLA include:

# whether UG is available to the adult L2 learner to guide acquisition and to what extent;
# whether L2 learners can reset [[Principles and parameters|linguistic parameters]] from their L1;
# whether second-language learners experience difficulties [[Interface hypothesis|interfacing between different modules]] of the grammar;
# whether child second language acquisition differs from that of adults.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":6">Jabballa, M.M.H (2017). Access to Second Language Acquisition. ''Journal of Language Studies, Semiotics and Semantics,'' Vol. 8 (2) 479-484</ref>

As generative second language research endeavours to explain the totality of L2 acquisition phenomena, it is also concerned with investigating the extent of [[Language transfer|linguistic transfer]], [[Critical period hypothesis|maturational effects]] on acquisition, and why some learners fail to acquire a target-like L2 grammar even with abundant input.<ref name=":1" /> Furthermore, studying L2 acquisition through a generative lens give linguists a better idea of the natural constraints on human languages and the inner workings of Universal Grammar.<ref name="b_haznedar">Haznedar, B. (2013). Child second language acquisition from a generative perspective. In Linguistic Approaches to Bilingualism (pp. 26–47). John Benjamins Publishing Company.</ref>

Research in generative second-language acquisition is presented at a range of conferences, including: GASLA (Generative Approaches to Second Language Acquisition), GALANA (Generative Approaches to Language Acquisition - North America), and BUCLD (Boston University Conference on Language Development).

Prominent researchers of the topic include [[Suzanne Flynn]] of [[MIT]], [[Bonnie Schwartz]] of [[University of Hawaii]], [[Antonella Sorace]] of [[University of Edinburgh]], and [[Lydia White]] of [[McGill University]].

==History==
=== Pre-GenSLA: 1960s-1970s ===
In the late 1960s-early 1970s researchers observed that the language and errors of L2 learners were not random but systematic and evidence of rule-governed behaviour.<ref>Adjémian, C. (1976). On the nature of interlanguage systems. Language Learning, 26, 297–320.</ref><ref>Corder, S. P. (1967). The significance of learners’ errors. International Review of Applied Linguistics, 5, 161–70.</ref><ref>Nemser, W. (1971).  Approximative systems of foreign language learners. International Review of Applied Linguistics, 9, 115–23.</ref><ref>Selinker, L. (1972). Interlanguage.International Review of Applied Linguistics, 10, 209–31.</ref> From this observation researchers proposed the concept of [[interlanguage]] which refers to the language system used by L2 learners that contains interacting linguistic aspects of both the L1 and L2. This system theory regarding the interlanguage suggests that L2 learners have mental grammars that can be described with rules and principles.<ref name=":2" />

===The Beginnings of GenSLA:1980-1990s===
The history of GenSLA research begins in the 1980s prompted by two interconnected questions:

# The logical problem of language acquisition
# How the logical problem of language acquisition applies to L2 acquisition in adulthood.<ref name=":1" />

The logical problem of language acquisition refers to the observable mismatch between the primary linguistic data (PLD) or language specific input a child is exposed to and the state of their eventual language system, that is, children appear to acquire their native language quickly and with little negative feedback even when the input is uneven, inconsistent and unrepresentative of their ultimate linguistic competence.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> Some suggest in an argument commonly known as [[Poverty of the stimulus|Poverty of the Stimulus]] (POS) that there are, in fact, certain properties of language that are too abstract, subtle and complex to be acquired by language input and the operation of domain general cognitive mechanisms alone. Similarly, children are not exposed to a rich wealth of linguistic data to be able to acquire all the rules and principles of their distinct language.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book|title=The Cambridge Handbook of Second Language Acquisition|last=Herschensohn|first=Julia|publisher=Cambridge University Press|date=July 2013|isbn=978-1-107-00771-0|location=Cambridge, UK|pages=137–158}}</ref> Therefore, an extra component, such as the UG which consists of innate domain-specific linguistic knowledge, is needed to account for these POV properties.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

Subsequently, starting from the assumption of UG GenSLA researchers asked how the problem of language acquisition applies to L2 acquisition in adulthood. This encompassed questions about what similarities and differences exist between child L1 acquisition and adult L2 acquisition and, in particular, whether or not adults also have access UG.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":6" /> Indeed, most theories and research in the first two decades of GenSLA actually revolved around this singular question to which there are four proposed answers:

# L2 learners have direct or full access to UG
# L2 learners have partial access to UG
#L2 learners have indirect access to UG
# L2 learners have no access to UG.

GenSLA researchers assumed during these early decades that if they could show that a particular POS property operated or did not operate in L2 grammar they could generalize to other POS properties and to UG accessibility or non-accessibility in general.<ref name=":2" /> Because an L2 learner's L1 contains UG information available for transfer to their L2 it was thought that the strongest case for L2 access to UG would be evidence of knowledge in L2 learners that constituted instances of POS properties that are non-transferable. In other words, linguistic knowledge that could not be learned from L2 input, explicit learning, transfer from L1 knowledge, or the operation of domain general cognitive mechanisms.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

===Feature Focused: Late 1990s-early 2000s===
The field of GenSLA research experienced significant theoretical developments in the late 1990s/early 2000s following changes in generative linguistic theory inspired by [[Noam Chomsky|Chomsky]]'s [[minimalist program]]. These changes shifted the debate from questions solely about access to UG to the consideration of specific features in L2 grammars and how they are represented.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> The features under consideration here are linguistic units that reflect grammatical meanings such as [[Grammatical tense|tense]], [[Grammatical case|case]], [[Grammatical number|number]], [[Grammatical person|person]], [[Grammatical gender|gender]], or conceptual meanings such as [[evidentiality]], [[habitual aspect]] and [[definiteness]]<ref name=":1" />

One key characteristic of these features is that they reflect variation across languages in their overtness, which became particularly important to GenSLA research.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> A feature of a word or phrase is said to be overt if there is surface evidence of its existence within that word or phrase. By contrast, a feature of a word or phrase is said to be covert if there is no surface evidence of its existence within that word or phrase. This made interesting predictions about adult L2 learning behaviour, for example, that L2 overt [[morphology (linguistics)|morphology]] should be easier to acquire if the learner has similar overt features in their L1.<ref name=":1" /> In one relevant study it was shown that Russian but not Japanese L2 learners of English were, in line with these predictions, reliably sensitive to English plural errors, (Russian has overt plural morphology while Japanese does not).<ref>Jiang, N., Novokshanova, E., Masuda, K., & Wang, X. (2011). Morphological congruency and the acquisition of L2 morphemes. Language Learning, 61, 940–967.</ref>

Another important element of these features for GenSLA research is interpretability.<ref name=":1" /> A feature is said to be interpretable if it contributes to sentence meaning and uninterpretable if it has grammatical significance only. This predicted that only meaningful features should be accessible to adult L2 learners and purely grammatical features should not be accessible for L1 transfer. No access and partial access theories sometimes adopted this distinction, arguing that it explains much variation attested in adult L2 grammars. For example, that Chinese speakers learning English as an L2 often omit third-person singular agreement morphology in obligatory contexts could easily be explained because these features are uninterpretable in Chinese.<ref>Li, Y. (1990). Order and constituency in Mandarin Chinese. Dordrecht, The Netherlands: Kluwer.</ref>

===New Populations: 2000s Onwards===
By the 2000s it was generally accepted that adult SLA differed from child L1 acquisition in process and typical outcomes and there was evidence for adult accessibility in at least some properties of UG. This motivated GenSLA theory to shift focus from questions just about UG accessibility and specific features to describing and explaining variation at group and individual levels.<ref name=":1" /> The last decade has also seen a significant increase in GenSLA studies that examine SLA in populations complementary to L2 acquisition including [[heritage language|heritage]] [[bilingualism]], child L2 acquisition, and [[multilingualism|multilingual]] acquisition to gain new insights into the latter.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> For example, it was found that heritage bilinguals diverge from [[monolingualism|monolinguals]] in the ultimate state of their eventual language system in ways similar to adult L2 learners even though they are native speakers and even when the learning process takes places in a naturalistic setting in early childhood.<ref name=rothman>Rothman, J., & Treffers-Daller, J. (2014). A prolegomenon to the construct of the native speaker: Heritage speaker bilinguals are natives too! Applied Linguistics, 35, 93–98.</ref> This casts doubt on the [[Critical period hypothesis|critical period]] hypothesis (CP) that age is the determining factor in convergent language acquisition, another rich area of debate in GenSLA research <ref>Montrul, S. (2008). Incomplete acquisition in bilingualism: Re-examining the age factor (Vol. 39). Amsterdam, the Netherlands: John Benjamins Publishing.</ref><ref>Montrul, S. (2012). Is the heritage language like a second language? EuroSLA Yearbook, 12, 1–29.</ref><ref>Montrul, S. (2016). Heritage language acquisition. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.</ref> With respect to child L2 acquisition, it was hypothesized that if child and adult L2 learners follow the same developmental path this would call into question the claims made by some GenSLA researchers that differences between L1 and L2 learners are due to the inaccessibility of UG. This is because in GenSLA child L2 learners under the age of 7 to 8 are hypothesized to have access to UG. Thus, if the developmental paths of child and adult L2 learners overlap significantly it is likely that the basis of difference is the shared experience they have with their L1. If, however, if they follow different developmental paths this would seem to support the claim that adult L2 learners do not have access to UG; their learning must instead be due to other factors.<ref name=":1" /> Finally, in multilingual acquisition, if it were shown that adult L2 learners can transfer POS properties only available from their L2 to their L3 or L4 etc. this could also be used to cast doubt on the CP hypothesize.<ref name=":1" />

In addition, there has been a movement towards examining children's L2 acquisition. The study of child SLA is argued to be an important way of examining both child L1 acquisition and adult L2 acquisition.<ref name="b_haznedar" /> Unlike adults, children acquiring an L2 are considered to have full and direct access to Universal Grammar, and are typically more successful at retainment of the L2 and reaching a state of fluency.<ref name="b_haznedar" /> Some scholars have argued that examining child L2 acquisition is an essential tool in solving the debate over adult access to UG <ref name="b_haznedar" /> Most recent work on child L2 acquisition within generative framework has focused on the following 3 major issues:

# L1 influence in child L2 acquisition,
# The availability of functional categories (emphasis on the acquisition of tense-agreement and tense-aspect),
# Morphological variability.<ref name="b_haznedar" />

==Access Theories==
[[File:Access Theories.png|thumb|Access Theories|250px]]

===No Access===
Theories of no access argue that adult second language learners do not have access to UG. One source of evidence for this position stems from research observations made in the 1970s and 80s that children experience a critical period or reduced ability over time to acquire a functional L1 morphosyntactic system that ends around puberty. L2 acquisition, however, does not share this similarity with late L1 acquisition, L2 learners being generally more successful than the latter. Additionally, child L2 and adult L2 learners differ greatly in the developmental paths they take and their ultimate attainment.<ref name=":1" />
 
The Fundamental Difference Hypothesis <ref>Bley-Vroman, R. (1990). [https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Robert_Bley-Vroman/publication/240412239_The_Logical_Problem_of_Foreign_Language_Learning/links/55671a8008aeccd777378100/The-Logical-Problem-of-Foreign-Language-Learning.pdf The logical problem of foreign language learning]. Linguistic Analysis 20: 3 – 49</ref> refers to how linguistic methods of language acquisition applied in early childhood are not available for adult learners, which points to a fundamental difference in access to UG between child and adult learners. Adult L2 acquisition resembles the process of general adult learning in fields where there is no domain-specific learning system believed to exist.

===Direct Access===
Theories of direct access argue that UG is still directly accessible to adult second language learners, in addition to syntactic property transfer from their L1.<ref name="b_haznedar" /> Evidence for this position stems from research observations that although child L1 and adult L2 grammars differ, adult L2 grammars do exhibit evidence of POS properties that cannot be linked to transfer from their native language or learning. For example, adult L2 learners show knowledge of parameter settings other than those of their first language. In direct access theories the differences between adult and children must subsequently be explained on the basis of something other than UG accessibility. Many propose that it is in fact this difference between the L1 initial state and L2 initial state that accounts for the differences, when comparing child SLA learners and adult SLA learners.<ref name="b_haznedar" /> Advocates of this position also frequently tried to show that learners are stuck within principles and parameter settings exemplified in their L1.<ref>Schachter, J. (1989). Testing a proposed universal. In S. Gass and J. Schachter (eds.), Linguistic perspectives on second language acquisition (pp. 73–88). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.</ref>

Some experts have commented that theories of direct access could also be characterized as direct access since the learner is not restricted only to UG principles and parameter settings of the L1 grammar due to the resetting and restructuring that occurs with the learning of the L2.<ref name="l_white" /><ref name=":7" /><ref name=":8" />

Some relevant theories that assume access to UG in adulthood and propose other factors as cause of differences between L1 and L2 acquisition: Missing Surface Inflection Hypothesis,<ref name="b_haznedar" /><ref>Prevost, P. and L. White. (2000). Missing surface inflection or impairment in second language acquisition? Evidence from tense and agreement. Second Language Research 16.</ref> Feature Reassembly Hypothesis,<ref>Lardiere, D. (2009). [https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/e647/d6942d85a47b0aeb4b715788296aa2ee10a3.pdf Some thoughts on the contrastive analysis of features in second language acquisition]. Second Language Research, 25, 173–227.</ref> Prosodic Transfer Hypothesis,<ref>Slabakova, R. (2008). Meaning in the second language. Berlin: Walter de Gruyter.</ref> Interface Hypothesis.<ref>Sorace, A. (2000). Introduction: Syntactic optionality in non-native grammars. Second Language Research, 16, 93–102.</ref><ref>Sorace, A. (2011). [https://www.jbe-platform.com/content/journals/10.1075/lab.1.1.01sor Pinning down the concept of “interface” in bilingualism]. Linguistic Approaches to Bilingualism, 1, 1–33.</ref><ref>Sorace, A., & Filiaci, F. (2006). Anaphora resolution in near-native speakers of Italian. Second Language Research, 22, 339–368.</ref>

===Indirect Access ===
The indirect access viewpoint considers the possibility that access to a second language grammar is first through a first language, where the second language then causes a resetting and restructuring of the learners understanding of grammar once they have been exposed to the second language.<ref name="l_white">White, L. (2003). [https://books.google.com/books?id=2Q25nuAQ-oQC Second Language Acquisition and Universal Grammar]. Cambridge Textbooks in Linguistics. (pp. 15-17)</ref>

===Partial Access===
Theories of partial access argue that L2 learners have partial, but not full access to UG through their L1.<ref name="l_white" /><ref name=":7">Thomas, M. (1991b). [https://www.jstor.org/stable/415105 Universal Grammar and the interpretation of reflexives in a second language]. Language 67: 211–39.</ref><ref name=":8">Thomas, M. (1993). [https://books.google.com/books?id=ATZCAAAAQBAJ Knowledge of reflexives in a second language]. Amsterdam: John Benjamins.</ref>

==Generative SLA in the Classroom==

=== Teaching ESL ===
Scholars in Generative SLA have suggested that their research is relevant in developing effective methods of teaching a second language in classroom settings including bilingual, immersion, second dialect education and second language literacy programs.<ref name="s_sutherland">Sutherland, S. (2015). A Generative Second Language Acquisition-Inspired Syllabus: An Article Remedial Course for Intermediate Adult ESL Learners. The University of Sheffield.</ref> Practical GenSLA researchers seek to go beyond "passive" acquisition and utilize theories in SLA to efficiently teach L2s.<ref name="whong" /> The practical application of GenSLA is based on what is necessary or unnecessary to teach based on UG access.<ref name="s_sutherland" /><ref name="whong" /> For example, it is generally accepted that [[preposition and postposition|prepositional]] [[grammatical modifiers|modifiers]] can be accessed in UG, and because of this it may not be efficient to teach them explicitly.<ref name="s_sutherland" /> However, other grammatical issues such as [[topic and comment|topic and focus]] structures are not innate, and therefore second language learners benefit from explicit teaching.<ref name="s_sutherland" /> Additionally, GenSLA research can be used with relation to topics processing, practice and [[orthography]], and be extended beyond mere production.<ref name="whong">Whong, M., Gil, K.H., Marsden, H. (2013). Introduction: Generative Second Language Acquisition and Language Pedagogy. In Universal Grammar and the Second Language Classroom. Educational Linguistics. 1-13.</ref> Research on practical, educational usage of GenSLA theories have been explored in L2s such as Spanish, English, German and French.<ref name="whong" />

=== Aiding Populations with Special Language Learning Needs ===
It has been suggested that GenSLA research could be used to aid populations with special language learning needs, for example, it might be used to develop language intervention programs using methods similar to those implemented in second language teaching to help children with [[down syndrome]]<ref name=Mahoney>Mahoney, G. 1975: Ethnological approach to delayed language acquisition. American Journal of Mental Deficiency, 80, 139–48.</ref><ref>Rosenberg, S. (1982). The language of the mentally retarded: development processes and intervention. In S. Rosenberg (ed.), Handbook of Applied Psycholinguistics: Major Thrusts of Research and Theory.Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates, 329–92.Rutherford, W. E. (ed.)</ref> or [[Alzheimer's disease|Alzheimers]] patients.<ref name=Hyltenstam>Hyltenstam, K. and Stroud, C. 1993: Second language regression in Alzheimer's dementia. In K.Hyltenstam and A. Viberg (eds), Progression and Regression in Language:Sociocultural, Neuropsychological and Linguistic Perspectives. Cambridge:Cambridge University Press, 222– 42.</ref> Insights from GenSLA could also help multilingual children by ensuring educators not confuse problems of second language acquisition with [[Learning disability|learning disabilities]],<ref name=Cummins>Cummins, J. (1984). Bilingualism and Special Education: Issues on Assessment and Pedagogy. Clevedon: Multilingual Matters.</ref> bilinguals undergoing primary [[language attrition|language loss]]<ref>Seliger, H. W. (1996). Primary language attrition in the context of bilingualism. In W. R. Ritchie and T. J. Bhatia (eds), Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. San Diego: Academic Press, 605–26.</ref><ref>Seliger, H. W. and Vago, R. M. (eds) (1991). First Language Attrition. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.</ref> or deaf and hearing children learning sign language as a first or second language.<ref name=Brent>Berent, G. P. (1996). The acquisition of English syntax by deaf learners. In W. R. Ritchie and T. J. Bhatia (eds), Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. San Diego: Academic Press, 469–506.</ref><ref>Mayberry, R. 1993: [http://grammar.ucsd.edu/labs/mayberrylab/papers/Mayberry93.pdf First-language acquisition after childhood differs from second-language acquisition:the case of American Sign Language].Journal of Speech and Hearing Research,36, 1258–70.</ref><ref>Strong, M. (ed.) 1988: Language Learning and Deafness. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.</ref>

== Applications: Word Order Acquisition ==
There have been debates regarding how one can apply the principles of Generative L2 Acquisition to individuals acquiring a second language with a different [[word order]] from their L1 (for example individuals whose L1 was [[Subject–object–verb|SOV]] and are now learning a [[Subject–verb–object|SVO]] language, or vice versa). Some researchers have hypothesized that on the basis of the full transfer full access theory, individuals will use L1 grammar and parameter setting initially during their acquisition of L2, but would still have access to the UG. This notion contains features of both the direct and indirect theories of UG, which involves some form of access to the UG. However, research has shown that not all individuals acquiring the L2 will produce transfers from their L1, as the transfer process depends on the structural components of the L1. Instead, some linguists have argued that the process of second language acquisition can be accounted for by general learning principles and in fact not does correspond to having access to the UG. Therefore, this particular issue of different word-order acquisition can be used to call into question if the direct access theory of UG is relevant to second language acquisition, or if a no access theory is more plausible.<ref>Pierantozzi, Cristina. 2009. [http://www.lingref.com/cpp/gasla/10/paper2275.pdf The Acquisition of Word Order in Different Learner Types]. In Proceedings of the 10th Generative Approaches to Second Language Acquisition Conference (GASLA 2009), ed. Melissa Bowles et al., 264-271. Somerville, MA: Cascadilla Proceedings Project.</ref>

==Criticism==
There has been some criticism regarding Generative L2 Acquisition on the basis of methodology and other linguistic theories.

===Methodological Issues===
There have been claims that there are several methodological issues in generative research. The subjects need to have a requisite level of the L2 to see if a principle is operating in their interlanguage grammar. Furthermore, complex structures are often needed to test for interlanguage grammar, and the speakers need to be able to competently engage with the structures within their current L2 capacity. It is also difficult to rule out the influence of the L1, if the languages present similar principles that are in question. One of the most controversial methodological issues in generative second language acquisition is regarding what L2 data is collected. There is a need to obtain information about competence rather than performance, and it is difficult to obtain samples which contain the complex structures necessary to observe UG-related parameters and principles. Elicited data is preferred, but still problematic based on the skill level of the speaker, and is not considered naturally occurring speech.<ref name="ellis_r" />

===The Minimal Tree Hypothesis===
[[File:Stages in Minimal Trees Hypothesis.png|thumb|Stages in Minimal Trees Hypothesis|250px]]
The Minimal Trees Hypothesis (MTH) is a highly debated hypothesis which is concerned with the distinction between [[Functional item|Functional Categories]] and [[Functional item|Lexical Categories]] during language transfer.<ref name="vanikka">Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (1994). Direct Access to X-bar Theory: Evidence from Korean and Turkish Adults Learning German. Language Acquisition Studies in Generative Grammar: Papers in Honor of Kenneth Wexler from the GLOW 1991 Workshops, ed. by T. Hoekstra and B.D. Schwarts, 265-316. Direct Access to X-bar Theory: Evidence from Korean and Turkish Adults Learning German. Language Acquisition Studies in Generative Grammar: Papers in Honor of Kenneth Wexler from the GLOW 1991 Workshops, ed. by T. Hoekstra and B.D. Schwarts, 265-316.</ref><ref name="b_haznedar" /> Based on a study of adult SLA learners of German, Korean and Turkish, this hypothesis asserts that only lexical categories transfer from the L1, and functional categories develop over time.<ref name="vanikka" /><ref name="b_haznedar" /> This development has also been termed "organic grammar", in which the development of functional categories develop from Verb Phrase (VP)→ Inflection Phrase (IP)→ Complementizer Phrase (CP).<ref>Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (2006). The roots of syntax and how they grow: Organic grammar, the basic variety and processability theory. In Paths of Development in L1 and L2 Acquisition: In honor of Bonnie D. Schwartz, ed. S. Unsworth et al. 77-106.</ref><ref>Haznedar, B. (2013)</ref> The phases have been termed the "Bare VP Stage", the "Underspecified VP Stage" and the "Agr-P Stage" <ref name="y_tomita">Tomita, Y. (2001). On the Validity of the Minimal Trees Hypothesis. English Linguistics, 18(1), 271–294.</ref>  The controversy surrounding the MTH has to do with methodological problems and theoretical problems which emerge in the hypothesis.<ref name="y_tomita" /> With regard to the methodological problems, the MTH has issue pertaining to ''performance'' vs. ''competence'' in data collection.<ref name="y_tomita" /> The theoretical problems which exist in the hypothesis relate to the role of input, the transfer of lexical categories, and the development of previous linguistic theories and research regarding L2 Acquisition research.<ref name="y_tomita" /> The theoretical basis of the MTH has been contested by many researchers, which call into question the validity of the hypothesis.<ref name="y_tomita" /> Vainikka & Young-Scholten themselves, the originators of the hypothesis, acknowledge that their theory is more "radical" than what is often seen in generative SLA academia.<ref>Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (1994). Direct Access to X-bar Theory: Evidence from Korean and Turkish Adults Learning German. Language Acquisition Studies in Generative Grammar: Papers in Honor of Kenneth Wexler from the GLOW 1991 Workshops, ed. by T. Hoekstra and B.D. Schwarts, 265-316.</ref> Despite the controversial nature of the hypothesis, MTH has been considered an extremely strong and valuable contribution to SLA research and generative grammar as a whole.<ref name="y_tomita" />

===The Logical Problem of Acquisition===
Some researchers deny the existence of any domain specific linguistic knowledge.<ref>
*Bybee, J. (2010). Language, usage and cognition. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.
*Evans, V. (2014). The language myth: Why language is not an instinct. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.
*Gerken, L., Wilson, R., & Lewis, W. (2005). Infants can use distributional cues to form syntactic categories.Journal of Child Language, 32, 249–268.
*Goldberg, A. (2013). Constructionist approaches to language. In Thomas Hoffmann & Graeme Trousdale (Eds.), Handbook of construction grammar. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>
*Gries, S. T. (2012). Frequencies, probabilities, and association measures in usage-/exemplar-based linguistics:Some necessary clarifications. Studies in Language, 36, 477–510.
*O’Grady, W. (2005). Syntactic carpentry: An emergentist approach to syntax. New York: Routledge.
*Redington, M., Charter, N., & Finch, S. (1998). Distributional information: A powerful cue for acquiring syntactic categories. Cognitive Science, 22, 425–469.
*Tomasello, M. (2003). Constructing a language: A usage-based theory of language acquisition. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press.</ref> They contest the existence of the logical problem of acquisition and the existence of UG hypothesized to fill the alleged [[explanatory gap]]. If this is true it would throw the generative approach to SLA into question. Supporters of GenSLA argue, however, that in order to disprove the logical problem of acquisition detractors would have to either show there are no instances of poverty of stimulus properties or when input alone is insufficient, one needs to explain the child's resulting competence in virtue of the operation of domain general cognitive mechanisms, [[Statistical learning in language acquisition|statistical learning]] or processing considerations.<ref>Schwartz, B. D., & Sprouse, R. (2013). Generative approaches and the poverty of the stimulus. In J. Herschensohn & M. Young-Scholten (Eds.), The Cambridge handbook of second language acquisition (pp. 137–58). Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.</ref><ref>Valian, V. (2014). Arguing about innateness. Journal of Child Language, 41, 78.</ref> They subsequently point to the fact that this has not yet been attempted exhaustively and no parsimonious alternatives have been offered to explain how poverty of stimulus properties are acquired.<ref name=":1">Rothman, J., & Slabakova, R. (2018). [https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/C73C9D3F290EFE235B3F0CB0970A238D/S0272263117000134a.pdf/div-class-title-the-generative-approach-to-sla-and-its-place-in-modern-second-language-studies-div.pdf The Generative Approach to SLA and its place in Modern Second Language Studies]. ''Studies in Second Language Acquisition'', ''20'', 417–442.</ref> The logical problem of language acquisition is thought to prevail so long as there are any poverty of stimulus properties that cannot otherwise be accounted for.

==Beyond L2==
The recent decades have witnessed an expansion of L2 field to L3 acquisition. The main researchers and publishing venues remained the same while the number of models and hypotheses regarding multilingual language acquisition have soared. Nearly all of them are nested in generative linguistics. There are three main groups of models: partial [[Language transfer|transfer]], wholesale transfer, and those rejecting transfer. The key question on which the models differ is what the source of [[Language transfer|linguistic transfer]] is, and what the role of previous languages are. 
{| class="wikitable"
|+
!Model
!Proposal
!Key references
|-
| colspan="3" |''Partial transfer''
|-
|Full Transfer Potential
|The initial state of the L2 is the L1 plus the [[Language acquisition device|genetic endowment for language acquisition]]:
• The complete grammar of the L1 remains active/accessible (so no need to make a copy of it).

• L2 (like L1) is learning by [[parsing]]: If there is an identical or similar "micro-cue" available in the L1, this will be used to parse the L2; If there is no similar structure in the L1 grammar, the learner resorts to [[Universal grammar|Universal Grammar]]

• Any property of the L1 may transfer. Transfer takes place property by property.
|See [[Marit Westergaard]];<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Westergaard |first=Marit |date=July 2021 |title=Microvariation in multilingual situations: The importance of property-by-property acquisition |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0267658319884116 |journal=Second Language Research |language=en |volume=37 |issue=3 |pages=379–407 |doi=10.1177/0267658319884116 |issn=0267-6583|hdl=10037/16671 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
|-
|Linguistic Proximity Model
|Ln acquisition involves incremental property-by-property learning and allows for both facilitative and non-facilitative influence (transfer) from one or both previously acquired languages. Crosslinguistic influence occurs when a particular linguistic property in the Ln input reveals abstract structural similarity with linguistic properties of the previously learned languages.
|See [[Marit Westergaard]];<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Westergaard |first1=Marit |last2=Mitrofanova |first2=Natalia |last3=Mykhaylyk |first3=Roksolana |last4=Rodina |first4=Yulia |date=December 2017 |title=Crosslinguistic influence in the acquisition of a third language: The Linguistic Proximity Model |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1367006916648859 |journal=International Journal of Bilingualism |language=en |volume=21 |issue=6 |pages=666–682 |doi=10.1177/1367006916648859 |issn=1367-0069|hdl=10037/10725 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
|-
|Scalpel Model
|The activated grammatical possibilities of the L1-plus-L2 combined grammar act with a scalpel-like precision, rather than as a blunt object, to extract the enhancing, or facilitative, options of L1 or L2 grammars. However, additional factors such as processing complexity, misleading input, and construction frequency in the target L3 are also operative property by property. 
|See [[Roumyana Slabakova|Romyana Slabakova]];<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Slabakova |first=Roumyana |date=December 2017 |title=The scalpel model of third language acquisition |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1367006916655413 |journal=International Journal of Bilingualism |language=en |volume=21 |issue=6 |pages=651–665 |doi=10.1177/1367006916655413 |issn=1367-0069|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
|-
| colspan="3" |''Rejection of transfer''
|-
|Cumulative-Enhancement Model
|CEM proposes that language learning is guided by the principle "the more languages you learn, the easier it gets". The authors suggest that language learning is '''fundamentally cumulative''' and all previous language knowledge is available for a language learner: for example, in learning L3, both L1 and L2 are accessible.  
|See [[Suzanne Flynn]];<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Flynn |first1=Suzanne |last2=Foley |first2=Claire |last3=Vinnitskaya |first3=Inna |date=January 2004 |title=The Cumulative-Enhancement Model for Language Acquisition: Comparing Adults' and Children's Patterns of Development in First, Second and Third Language Acquisition of Relative Clauses |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/14790710408668175 |journal=International Journal of Multilingualism |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=3–16 |doi=10.1080/14790710408668175 |issn=1479-0718|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Citation |last1=Berkes |first1=Éva |title=Third Language Acquisition in Adulthood |date=2012 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1075/sibil.46.11ber |pages=143–164 |access-date=2023-04-19 |place=Amsterdam |publisher=John Benjamins Publishing Company |last2=Flynn |first2=Suzanne|chapter=Further evidence in support of the Cumulative-Enhancement Model |series=Studies in Bilingualism |volume=46 |doi=10.1075/sibil.46.11ber |isbn=978-90-272-4187-0 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Umejima |first1=Keita |last2=Flynn |first2=Suzanne |last3=Sakai |first3=Kuniyoshi L. |date=2021-03-31 |title=Enhanced activations in syntax-related regions for multilinguals while acquiring a new language |journal=Scientific Reports |language=en |volume=11 |issue=1 |pages=7296 |doi=10.1038/s41598-021-86710-4 |issn=2045-2322 |pmc=8012711 |pmid=33790362}}</ref>
|-
|The Intermediate
|The Intermediate rejects the notion of transfer and proposes that linguistic representation are shared between languages via a consolidating function, thus better accounting for such phenomena as [[Bilingual interactive activation plus|co-activation]], [[code-switching]], and cumulativity of language learning (see the model above). The languages are treated as distinct [[Set theory|sets]], but as sets with nonempty [[intersection]]s. More fundamentally, The Intermediate also rejects linguistic [[Linguistic typology|typology]] and typological proximity.
|<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ozernyi |first=Daniil M. |date=2022-05-05 |title=Some remarks on the history of transfer in language studies |journal=Proceedings of the Linguistic Society of America |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=5206 |doi=10.3765/plsa.v7i1.5206 |issn=2473-8689|doi-access=free }}</ref>
|-
| colspan="3" |''Wholesale transfer''
|-
|L1 Privilege Model
|L1 plays the key role in acquisition of any subsequent language; this is attributed solely to the factor that L1 is the native language.
|<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hermas |first=Abdelkader |date=2014-07-03 |title=Restrictive Relatives in L3 English: L1 Transfer and Ultimate Attainment Convergence |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/07268602.2014.898227 |journal=Australian Journal of Linguistics |volume=34 |issue=3 |pages=361–387 |doi=10.1080/07268602.2014.898227 |issn=0726-8602|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
|-
|L2 Status Model
|L2 Status claims that L2 will play the key role in subsequent language acquisition because whereas L1 was acquired in infancy, L2 and L3 share the mode of acquisition—in adulthood. This similarity makes L2 the key source for L3 regardless of typology or anything else.
|<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Falk |first1=Ylva |last2=Bardel |first2=Camilla |date=January 2011 |title=Object pronouns in German L3 syntax: Evidence for the L2 status factor |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0267658310386647 |journal=Second Language Research |language=en |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=59–82 |doi=10.1177/0267658310386647 |issn=0267-6583|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Citation |last1=Bardel |first1=Camilla |title=The L2 status factor and the declarative/procedural distinction |date=2012 |url=https://benjamins.com/catalog/sibil.46.06bar |work=Studies in Bilingualism |volume=46 |pages=61–78 |editor-last=Cabrelli Amaro |editor-first=Jennifer |access-date=2023-04-19 |place=Amsterdam |publisher=John Benjamins Publishing Company |language=en |doi=10.1075/sibil.46.06bar |isbn=978-90-272-4187-0 |last2=Falk |first2=Ylva |editor2-last=Flynn |editor2-first=Suzanne |editor3-last=Rothman |editor3-first=Jason}}</ref>
|-
|Language of Community
|Dominant spoken or signed language in a community of speakers serves as the source of transfer for L3.
|<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fallah |first1=Nader |last2=Jabbari |first2=Ali Akbar |last3=Fazilatfar |first3=Ali Mohammad |date=April 2016 |title=Source(s) of syntactic cross-linguistic influence (CLI): The case of L3 acquisition of English possessives by Mazandarani–Persian bilinguals |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0267658315618009 |journal=Second Language Research |language=en |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=225–245 |doi=10.1177/0267658315618009 |issn=0267-6583|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
|-
|Typological Proximity Model
|TPM is based on the notion of psycho([[Linguistic typology|typology]]): between L1 and L2, whichever language is "closer" to L3 serves as a substrate for L3. TPM hypothesizes that the entire grammar of either L1 or L2 is transferred to serve as the basis for L3. Language learner then proceeds by error-driven revision. 
|<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rothman |first=Jason |date=January 2011 |title=L3 syntactic transfer selectivity and typological determinacy: The typological primacy model |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0267658310386439 |journal=Second Language Research |language=en |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=107–127 |doi=10.1177/0267658310386439 |issn=0267-6583|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rothman |first=Jason |date=April 2015 |title=Linguistic and cognitive motivations for the Typological Primacy Model (TPM) of third language (L3) transfer: Timing of acquisition and proficiency considered* |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bilingualism-language-and-cognition/article/abs/linguistic-and-cognitive-motivations-for-the-typological-primacy-model-tpm-of-third-language-l3-transfer-timing-of-acquisition-and-proficiency-considered/56606CE09ED7FEA6DA3F2437C6D31B94 |journal=Bilingualism: Language and Cognition |language=en |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=179–190 |doi=10.1017/S136672891300059X |issn=1366-7289|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rothman |first=Jason |date=2010-07-01 |title=On the typological economy of syntactic transfer: Word order and relative clause high/low attachment preference in L3 Brazilian Portuguese |journal=Iral - International Review of Applied Linguistics in Language Teaching |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/iral.2010.011/html |language=en |volume=48 |issue=2–3 |pages=245–273 |doi=10.1515/iral.2010.011 |issn=1613-4141|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
|}

===Sources===
*Bley-Vroman, R. (1990). The logical problem of foreign language learning. Linguistic Analysis 20: 3 – 49 
*Doughty, C.J., Long, M.H. (2003). The Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. Oxford: Blackwell Publishing. 
*Gass, S. M. and Selinker, L. (2001): Second Language Acquisition: An Introductory Course. Second edition. Mahwah, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.
*Gregg, K. R. (1994): Second language acquisition: history and theory. Encyclopedia of Language and Linguistics.Second edition. Oxford: Pergamon, 3720–6
*Haznedar, B. (2013). Child second language acquisition from a generative perspective. In Linguistic Approaches to Bilingualism (pp.&nbsp;26–47). John Benjamins Publishing Company.
*Haznedar, B. and B. D. Schwartz. (1997). Are there optional infinitives in child L2 acquisition? In E. Hughes, M. Hughes and A. Greenhill (eds.), Proceedings of the 21st Annual Boston University Conference on Language Development. Somerville, MA: Cascadilla Press.
*Jordens, P. and Lalleman, J. (eds) (1988):Language Development. Dordrecht:Foris.
*Lardiere, D. (2009). Some thoughts on the contrastive analysis of features in second language acquisition. Second Language Research, 25, 173–227.
*Montrul, S. (2009). Reexamining the fundamental difference hypothesis: What can early bilinguals tell us?. The fundamental difference hypothesis 20 years later. 31(2). 
*Prevost, P. and L. White. (2000). Missing surface inflection or impairment in second language acquisition? Evidence from tense and agreement. Second Language Research 16.
*Ritchie, W. R. and Bhatia, T. J. (eds) (1996): Handbook of Second Language Acquisition. San Diego: Academic Press.
*Rothman, J., & Slabakova, R. (2018). The Generative Approach to SLA and its place in Modern Second Language Studies. Studies in Second Language Acquisition, 20(2018), 417–442.
*Schachter, J. (1988). Second language acquisition and its relationship to Universal Grammar. Applied Linguistics.
*Schachter, J. (1989). Testing a proposed universal. In S. Gass and J. Schachter (eds.), Linguistic perspectives on second language acquisition (pp.&nbsp;73–88). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
*Slabakova, R. (2008). Meaning in the second language. Berlin: Walter de Gruyter.
*Sorace, A. (2000). Introduction: Syntactic optionality in non-native grammars. Second Language Research, 16, 93–102.
*Sorace, A. (2011). Pinning down the concept of “interface” in bilingualism. Linguistic Approaches to Bilingualism, 1, 1–33.
*Sorace, A., & Filiaci, F. (2006). Anaphora resolution in near-native speakers of Italian. Second Language Research, 22, 339–368.
*Sutherland, S. (2015). A Generative Second Language Acquisition-Inspired Syllabus: An Article Remedial Course for Intermediate Adult ESL Learners. The University of Sheffield.
*Thomas, M. (1991b). Universal Grammar and the interpretation of reflexives in a second language. Language 67: 211–39.
*Thomas, M. (1993). Knowledge of reflexives in a second language. Amsterdam: John Benjamins.
*Towell, R. and Hawkins, R. (1994):Approaches to Second Language Acquisition. Clevedon: Multilingual Matters.
*Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (1994). Direct Access to X-bar Theory: Evidence from Korean and Turkish Adults Learning German. Language Acquisition Studies in Generative Grammar: Papers in Honor of Kenneth Wexler from the GLOW 1991 Workshops, ed. by T. Hoekstra and B.D. Schwarts, 265–316. 
*Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (1996). Gradual development of L2 phrase structure. Second Language Research - SECOND LANG RES. 12. 7-39. 10.1177/026765839601200102. 
*Vainikka, A. & Young-Scholten, M. (2006). The roots of syntax and how they grow: Organic grammar, the basic variety and processability theory. In Paths of Development in L1 and L2 Acquisition: In honor of Bonnie D. Schwartz, ed. S. Unsworth et al. 77–106. 
*White, L. (2003). Second Language Acquisition and Universal Grammar. Cambridge Textbooks in Linguistics. (pp.&nbsp;15–17).
*Whong, M., Gil, K.H., Marsden, H. (2013). Introduction: Generative Second Language Acquisition and Language Pedagogy. In Universal Grammar and the Second Language Classroom. Educational Linguistics. 1–13. 
{{SLA topics}}

[[Category:Generative linguistics|second-language acquisition]]
